timeline.event: Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) was born.
Debuted in the literary world with "The Celebrated Jumping Frog".
Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) died.

keyAchievements.title: American Author
keyAchievements.description: An American author more famously known by his pen name Mark Twain.

keyAchievements.title: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
keyAchievements.description: A children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

keyAchievements.title: The Celebrated Jumping Frog
keyAchievements.description: The work that made him debut in the literary world in 1865.

keyAchievements.title: The Innocents Abroad
keyAchievements.description: A work that gained popularity.

keyAchievements.title: Mississippi Trilogy
keyAchievements.description: Consists of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, Life on the Mississippi, and The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, expressing a hymn to the American and free spirit.

keyAchievements.title: Social Satires
keyAchievements.description: Works set in medieval feudal Europe, such as A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court, The Prince and the Pauper, and The Mysterious Stranger.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Christian Fundamentalism
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he published Letters from the Earth, criticizing Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Slavery
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he strove to expose and rectify the contradictions of a "free country with slavery."

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's real name?
faq.answer: Mark Twain's real name was Samuel Langhorne Clemens.

faq.question: What is Mark Twain's representative work?
faq.answer: His major works include The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, a children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

faq.question: Where did Mark Twain's pen name come from?
faq.answer: He used the unit of water depth, which he used during his time as a steamboat pilot on the Mississippi River, as his pen name.

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's religious stance?
faq.answer: Mark Twain was a progressive Presbyterian, but he criticized Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

timeline.event: Born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Lisbon earthquake.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Seven Years' War.
His romance with Gretchen was broken off.
Had a romance with Anna Katharina Schönkopf while studying at Leipzig University.
Wrote his first play, Die Laune des Verliebten (The Lover's Caprice).
Fostered pious religious feelings through his association with Susanne von Klettenberg while recovering at home.
Obtained a doctorate in law from the University of Strasbourg, reunited with Herder to open his eyes to the essence of literature, and laid the groundwork for the Sturm und Drang movement.
Opened a law practice.
Went to the Imperial Chamber Court in Wetzlar for legal training, where he met Charlotte Buff.
Published the play Götz von Berlichingen.
Published the novel The Sorrows of Young Werther.
Became engaged to Lili Schönemann, but broke it off shortly after.
Arrived in Weimar at the invitation of Duke Karl August of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach.
Handled state affairs for about 10 years in the early Weimar period, building a record of achievements as a privy councillor, senior privy councillor, and head of the cabinet.
Wrote the tragedy Iphigenia in Tauris (prose version).
Wrote Torquato Tasso.
Stated, "I am not an anti-Christian or an un-Christian, but a non-Christian."
Established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy, and wrote Iphigenia in Tauris (verse version).
Embarked on a journey to Italy lasting a year and a half.
Published Egmont.
Returned to Germany.
Began living with Christiane von Vulpius.
Had his eldest son, August.
Torquato Tasso was first published.
Followed Duke August in the War of the First Coalition and participated in the Battle of Valmy.
Participated in the Siege of Mainz.
Reunited with Schiller on his way back from a natural science conference in Jena and began their friendship.
Wrote the Bildungsroman Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship.
Wrote the epic poem Hermann and Dorothea.
Fell into great shock due to Schiller's death.
Completed Part I of Faust.
Completed Theory of Colours.
Wrote Italian Journey.
Wrote West-östlicher Divan.
Completed Wilhelm Meister's Journeyman Years.
Wrote the Marienbad Elegy.
Wrote Second Stay in Rome.
Completed Part II of Faust.
Passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: He completed German classical literature by creating numerous literary works such as The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

keyAchievements.title: Fostering Weimar as a Cultural Center
keyAchievements.description: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an Administrator and Scientist
keyAchievements.description: He participated in state affairs serving as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar, and left vast achievements showing interest in various natural science fields such as botany, anatomy, mineralogy, geology, and the theory of colors.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing German Classical Literature
keyAchievements.description: He established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy and met Schiller, blossoming German Weimar Classicism together.

keyAchievements.title: Court Theatre Director
keyAchievements.description: As the director of the court theatre, he was active across the board in management, directing, and actor training, elevating the theatre to a world-class level.

faq.question: Who was Goethe?
faq.answer: He was a German classical writer, philosopher, and scientist, who also served as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar.

faq.question: What are Goethe's representative works?
faq.answer: His representative works include The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

faq.question: What was Goethe's stance on religion?
faq.answer: Born into a Lutheran family, he developed doubts about his faith due to the Lisbon earthquake and the Seven Years' War, and referred to himself as a "non-Christian".

faq.question: What role did Goethe play in Weimar?
faq.answer: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center and participated in state affairs as an administrator.

timeline.event: Born in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria
Married Kim Ah-ryeo and had his first meeting with Kim Gu
His father Ahn Tae-hun converted to Catholicism
Converted to Catholicism at age 19 and received the baptismal name Thomas
Promoted Catholic missionary work and the establishment of a Catholic university
A dispute arose between his father Ahn Tae-hun and Seo Won-hun, a Qing Dynasty physician
Battered Seo Won-hun along with about 10 others including Lee Yong-il
Upon hearing the news of the Eulsa Treaty, he went to Shanghai for the movement to restore national sovereignty, but was refused cooperation and returned; his father Ahn Tae-hun passed away
After moving to Jinnampo, Pyongan-namdo, he managed a coal trading company before liquidating it and starting an education movement by establishing Samheung School

Took over Nampo Donui School and worked as a teacher; responded to the National Debt Repayment Movement and was appointed as the head of the Gwanseo branch of the National Debt Repayment Association; went to Primorsky Krai and joined the righteous army
Went into exile in Manchuria after the dissolution of the Korean Empire's army

Under Jeon Je-deok's command, as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army, Special Independent Commander, and Commander of the A-ryeong District, he and Eom In-seop led about 100 subordinates across the Tumen River to infiltrate the country and launch a surprise attack on the Japanese garrison
Formed the Dongui Danjihoe (Society of Cut Fingers) with 11 like-minded comrades and cut off a joint of his left ring finger to make a blood pledge
Left Vladivostok and moved to Harbin
Assassinated Ito Hirobumi in Harbin and was arrested by the Russian army
Sentenced to death at the Kwantung Government-General District Court in Lushun, Kwantung Leased Territory
Met with the Chief Justice of the High Court and raised an objection to being tried as a murderer for his actions as a righteous army commander
Received the sacrament of penance from Father Joseph Wilhelm
Offered his final Mass officiated by Father Wilhelm
His execution was postponed and he left his last requests to his two younger brothers
Executed at 10:00 AM at the age of 32
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Lieutenant General (later revised to the Republic of Korea Medal)

keyAchievements.title: Assassination of Ito Hirobumi
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the anti-Japanese independence movement by assassinating Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

keyAchievements.title: Education Movement
keyAchievements.description: Strived for the movement to restore national sovereignty through education by establishing Samheung School and taking over Nampo Donui School.

keyAchievements.title: Righteous Army Activities
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the righteous army movement after moving to Primorsky Krai in 1907, and engaged in combat with the Japanese army as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army in 1908.

keyAchievements.title: Formation of Dongui Danjihoe
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Dongui Danjihoe with 11 comrades in 1909 and cut off his left ring finger to strengthen his will for independence with a blood pledge.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Left an ideological legacy by writing the unfinished book "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

keyAchievements.title: Leaving Calligraphy
keyAchievements.description: Left many calligraphic works while imprisoned in Lushun Prison, some of which have been designated as national treasures.

faq.question: When and where was Ahn Jung-geun born?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was born on September 2, 1879, in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do, as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria.

faq.question: Whom did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

faq.question: Why did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate Ito Hirobumi?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Ito Hirobumi because he judged that Ito had committed 15 crimes, including the assassination of Empress Myeongseong, the signing of the Eulsa Treaty, and the dethronement of Emperor Gojong.

faq.question: When was Ahn Jung-geun executed?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was executed on March 26, 1910, following the assassination of Ito Hirobumi.

faq.question: Where are Ahn Jung-geun's remains?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's remains were buried in the prisoners' cemetery near Lushun Prison, but their exact location has not been found to this day.

faq.question: What are the major works of Ahn Jung-geun?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun wrote "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

faq.question: What is Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name is Thomas.

timeline.event: Born in Majae, Chobu-myeon, Gwangju-bu, Gyeonggi-do.
After his father Jeong Jae-won resigned from his government post and returned to his rural home, he named Jeong Yak-yong's childhood name Gwinong.
Married Pungsan Hong, the daughter of military official Hong Hwa-bo.
His father Jeong Jae-won became the assistant secretary of the Board of Taxation and rented a house in Seoul, where he met Lee Ga-hwan and Lee Seung-hun.
Participated in the Western Learning doctrine training session at Jueosa Temple and Cheonjinam Hermitage in Yangju, Gyeonggi-do, led by Gwon Cheol-sin.
Passed the Jeungwangsi examination celebrating the investiture of the Crown Prince and became a Saengwon (licentiate) through the Hoesi examination.
Heard an explanation of Catholic doctrines from Lee Byeok, the brother-in-law of his eldest brother Jeong Yak-hyeon.
The Myeongnyebang Incident occurred, where the Myeongnyebang community run by Lee Seung-hun was discovered and he was dragged to the Board of Punishments.
King Jeongjo defined Catholicism as an evil cult and issued a ban, and ordered the search and burning of Western Learning books related to Catholicism.
Passed the highest-level state examination at the age of 27 and entered government service.
When the Jinsan Incident occurred, where Yun Ji-chung held his mother's funeral in a Catholic manner in Jinsan, Jeolla-do, he was exiled to Haemi, Seosan, but was released after 11 days.
Participated in the design of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress and utilized the Geojunggi (traditional crane).
His father Jeong Jae-won passed away.
Lectured at Seonggyungwan and patrolled Yeoncheon, Saknyeong, etc. as a secret royal inspector of Gyeonggi-do.
Demoted to Geumjeong Chalbang (station master) due to the Eulmyo Persecution incident.
Due to suspicions raised by Busagwa Bak Jang-seol, Lee Seung-hun was demoted to Yesan, Lee Ga-hwan to Chungju Moksa, and Jeong Yak-yong to Hongju Geumjeong Chalbang.
While serving as the Third Minister of the Board of Punishments, he was impeached by his older brother Jeong Yak-jeon, so he submitted a "Jamyeongso" (petition of self-vindication) and resigned.
While living in retirement in his hometown, he heard the news of King Jeongjo's death and went up to Seoul.
Queen Dowager Jeongsun issued an order suppressing Catholicism and began a purge of the Namin faction.
Arrested and imprisoned, then his sentence was reduced to exile and he was released.
Began 18 years of exile in places like Janggi, Gyeongsang-do, and Gangjin, Jeolla-do.
His son Jeong Nong-jang (Nong-a) died of smallpox.
His exile was lifted and he rose to the position of Seungji (Royal Secretary).
Returned to his hometown.
While in exile in Gangjin-gun, Jeolla-do, he conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality.
Passed away at his home in Mahyeon-ri.
Posthumously promoted to Jeongheon Daebu Gyujanggak Jehak and granted the posthumous title 'Mundo'.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Writing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Known as the person who wrote the most books since the invention of Chinese characters, authoring over 500 volumes.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Mokminsimseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book that details the principles and mindset of local magistrates who govern the people.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Heumheumsinseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book detailing the principles and norms of judgments, punishments, and prison administration based on actual cases he investigated while serving as the magistrate of Goksan.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Gyeongseyupyo"
keyAchievements.description: A book discussing appropriate and standard institutional regulations for state management.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Magwahoetong"
keyAchievements.description: Authored the measles treatment book "Magwahoetong" based on Lee Heon-gil's "Majingibang," saving the lives of countless Joseon people.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Geojunggi
keyAchievements.description: Created the pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) for use during the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Silhak
keyAchievements.description: Synthesized the Silhak (Practical Learning) ideology handed down through Yu Hyeong-won and Seongho Yi Ik into a comprehensive system.

keyAchievements.title: Conception of Jeongjeon-ron
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality through the free distribution of land and communal labor-communal distribution.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure
keyAchievements.description: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012 to commemorate the 250th anniversary of his birth.

faq.question: When was Jeong Yak-yong born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was born on August 5, 1762, and passed away on April 7, 1836.

faq.question: What are the major works of Jeong Yak-yong?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong's three major works are "Mokminsimseo", "Heumheumsinseo", and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: What was Jeong Yak-yong's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was once deeply engrossed in Catholicism but apostatized after the Sinhae Persecution (1791), and actively participated in searching for Catholic believers during the Sinyu Persecution (1801).

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong do during his exile?
faq.answer: During his 18-year exile, Jeong Yak-yong authored extensive writings, including "Mokminsimseo" and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: How did Jeong Yak-yong contribute to the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress?
faq.answer: During the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress, Jeong Yak-yong created a pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) to utilize it.

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong's ideology aim for?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong aimed to establish Silhak of statecraft and practical use, correct distorted theories by returning to the essence of Confucianism of Confucius and Mencius, and build a welfare state that cares for the socially and economically weak.

faq.question: Was Jeong Yak-yong selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure?
faq.answer: Yes, Jeong Yak-yong was selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012, marking the 250th anniversary of his birth.

timeline.event: Born (estimated)
Died (estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Development of Confucian Thought
keyAchievements.description: Developed Confucius's thought and greatly influenced the transmission of Confucianism to future generations.

keyAchievements.title: The Book "Mencius"
keyAchievements.description: The book "Mencius" contains Mencius's thoughts, and it is believed to have been compiled by his disciples.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Innate Human Goodness
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the theory of innate human goodness, arguing that human nature is inherently good and that the human mind is equipped with the "Four Beginnings" of the four virtues: benevolence, righteousness, propriety, and wisdom.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Benevolent Government and Royal Politics
keyAchievements.description: After the age of 40, he traveled across the world advocating benevolent government and royal politics.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Four Beginnings
keyAchievements.description: Explained that the human mind contains the Four Beginnings: a feeling of compassion, a feeling of shame and dislike, a feeling of deference and compliance, and a feeling of right and wrong.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Five Relationships
keyAchievements.description: Presented the Theory of the Five Relationships, organizing five human relationships: intimacy between father and son, righteousness between ruler and minister, distinction between husband and wife, order between elder and junior, and trust between friends.

keyAchievements.title: Confirmation of the Right of Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed the right of revolution, stating that a monarch who betrays the will of the people and violates benevolence and righteousness is nothing but an ordinary fellow, and subduing such a monarch is not an act of regicide.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Cyclical View of History
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to prove the legitimacy of Confucius based on the cyclical view of history that a sage appears every 500 years.

faq.question: Is the story of Mencius's mother moving three times true?
faq.answer: The story of Mencius's mother moving three times is an anecdote from the "Biographies of Exemplary Women" written by the Han Dynasty scholar Liu Xiang, and the general consensus is that it is hard to view it as a historical fact.

faq.question: From whom did Mencius learn?
faq.answer: Mencius studied the Six Classics under the tutelage of Zisi, the grandson of Confucius.

faq.question: What was the name of Mencius's mother?
faq.answer: The name of Mencius's mother is Ji (伋).

faq.question: Who compiled the book "Mencius"?
faq.answer: There is a theory that the book "Mencius" was written in his later years, but it is more accurate to view it as having been compiled by his disciples.
